mnemonics.title: Remember Condition Flags
mnemonics.content: NZCV - Never Catch Vermin (Negative, Zero, Carry, Overflow) - think of them as 'bugs' that affect your code!

mnemonics.title: CMP Rule
mnemonics.content: CMP = SUB without STORE - performs subtraction, updates flags, throws away result

mnemonics.title: CMN Rule
mnemonics.content: CMN = ADD without STORE - adds operand, updates flags, throws away result

mnemonics.title: Signed vs Unsigned
mnemonics.content: Signed comparisons (GT/LT): Use N and V flags together. Unsigned (HI/LS): Use C and Z flags together

acronyms.usage: General term for status register in ARM architecture
Holds current processor state including flags and mode bits
Saves CPSR when exception occurs, restored on exception return
Subtracts operand from register, sets flags, discards result
Adds operand to register, sets flags, discards result
Bitwise AND operation, sets flags, tests if bits are set
Bitwise XOR operation, sets flags, tests bit patterns equal

visualTips.visualization: In CPSR bit order: bit 31=N, bit 30=Z, bit 29=C, bit 28=V. Think from MSB to LSB (NZCV spells a word!)
For CMP A,B: Result = A-B. If A<B (unsigned), borrow occurs → C=0. If A=B → Z=1. If (A-B) < 0 → N=1.
V flag set when: positive + positive = negative OR negative + negative = positive. Signs changed unexpectedly!
Group by: Equal tests (EQ/NE), Carry tests (CS/CC), Signed tests (GT/LT/GE/LE), Sign tests (MI/PL)

analogies.analogy: Like a car dashboard shows speed, fuel, warnings - CPSR shows processor status (flags), mode (gear), interrupts (warning lights)
CMP asks 'Are these equal?' or 'Is this bigger?' but doesn't change anything - just checks and updates the 'answer flags' in CPSR
SPSR is like an airbag - when exception (crash) happens, it saves the current state (CPSR) so it can be restored after recovery
N=Red (negative/stop), Z=Green (zero/go), C=Amber (carry/borrow), V=Red (overflow/error) - each tells you something about the result
